questions.stem: The serpentine, rope-like parallel arrangement of the stained bacilli shown is attributed to which virulence factor?

explanation.summary: Cord factor (trehalose dimycolate) causes M. tuberculosis to grow in the characteristic serpentine cords seen here and is toxic to mammalian cells while inhibiting neutrophil migration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cord factor (trehalose dimycolate) causes M. tuberculosis to grow in the characteristic serpentine cords seen here and is toxic to mammalian cells while inhibiting neutrophil migration.

questions.stem: This staining technique was used to visualize the pink bacilli shown against the blue background. What structural property of these bacteria makes this special stain necessary rather than a routine Gram stain?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A waxy, mycolic acid-rich cell wall that resists Gram staining

choices.label: E
choices.text: Complete absence of a cell wall

explanation.summary: Mycobacteria have a lipid-rich, mycolic acid cell wall that will not take up the Gram stain, so the Ziehl-Neelsen (acid-fast) stain is required; retained pink color identifies acid-fast bacilli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mycobacteria have a lipid-rich, mycolic acid cell wall that will not take up the Gram stain, so the Ziehl-Neelsen (acid-fast) stain is required; retained pink color identifies acid-fast bacilli.

questions.stem: This selective slant medium is used to culture the pathogen. Approximately how long does colony growth typically take here?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Several weeks, because the organism grows slowly

explanation.summary: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is a very slow grower on Lowenstein-Jensen medium, with detection taking up to 10 days or several weeks, reflecting its long doubling time.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is a very slow grower on Lowenstein-Jensen medium, with detection taking up to 10 days or several weeks, reflecting its long doubling time.

questions.stem: These rough, dry, buff-colored colonies with a waxy appearance are characteristic of which organism?

explanation.summary: The high mycolic acid content of the mycobacterial cell wall gives colonies their rough, waxy, buff appearance, distinct from the smooth colonies of typical bacteria.
explanation.whyCorrect: The high mycolic acid content of the mycobacterial cell wall gives colonies their rough, waxy, buff appearance, distinct from the smooth colonies of typical bacteria.

questions.stem: This chest radiograph accompanies primary tuberculosis. Which radiographic feature is most characteristic of primary TB, especially in a child?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hilar lymphadenopathy with a peripheral parenchymal focus

explanation.summary: Primary TB typically shows a peripheral parenchymal (Ghon) focus with prominent hilar lymphadenopathy, unlike the apical cavitation of reactivation disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary TB typically shows a peripheral parenchymal (Ghon) focus with prominent hilar lymphadenopathy, unlike the apical cavitation of reactivation disease.

questions.stem: This chest radiograph is from reactivation tuberculosis. In which lung zone do the cavitary lesions of reactivation TB characteristically appear?

explanation.summary: Reactivation TB characteristically produces cavitary disease in the well-oxygenated apices/upper lobes of the lungs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reactivation TB characteristically produces cavitary disease in the well-oxygenated apices/upper lobes of the lungs.

questions.stem: The photograph shows administration of the tuberculin (PPD) skin test, which is read 48-72 hours later. A positive reaction represents which type of hypersensitivity?

explanation.summary: A positive PPD is a type IV reaction: memory T cells sensitized by prior infection are recruited to the injection site, release cytokines, and cause induration over 48-72 hours.
explanation.whyCorrect: A positive PPD is a type IV reaction: memory T cells sensitized by prior infection are recruited to the injection site, release cytokines, and cause induration over 48-72 hours.

questions.stem: The organized structure diagrammed here walls off the infection. Which immune process is primarily responsible for forming it?

explanation.summary: Granuloma formation is driven by cell-mediated (type IV) immunity: T cells recruit and organize macrophages (including multinucleated giant cells) around the infection, walling it off with central caseous necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Granuloma formation is driven by cell-mediated (type IV) immunity: T cells recruit and organize macrophages (including multinucleated giant cells) around the infection, walling it off with central caseous necrosis.

questions.stem: This illustration depicts a peripheral lung lesion together with involved hilar lymph nodes. This combined finding is a hallmark of which stage of tuberculosis?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Latent infection with no visible lesion

explanation.summary: The Ghon complex (a peripheral parenchymal Ghon focus plus caseating hilar nodes) develops during primary tuberculosis infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Ghon complex (a peripheral parenchymal Ghon focus plus caseating hilar nodes) develops during primary tuberculosis infection.

questions.stem: This gross lung specimen shows innumerable small tan nodules scattered uniformly throughout the parenchyma. What mechanism produced this distribution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lymphatic spread limited to one hilum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct bronchial spread from a cavity

explanation.summary: Miliary tuberculosis results when bacilli seed the bloodstream and disseminate widely, producing tiny nodules throughout the lungs and other organs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Miliary tuberculosis results when bacilli seed the bloodstream and disseminate widely, producing tiny nodules throughout the lungs and other organs.

questions.stem: This schematic shows the macrophage-driven immune response to the organism, with a cytokine activating NK cells and driving naive T cells toward a helper phenotype. What is the principal downstream consequence of this activation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large-scale antibody production by plasma cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mast cell degranulation and histamine release

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enhanced macrophage killing of intracellular bacteria via IFN-gamma

explanation.summary: IL-12 from infected macrophages activates NK cells and drives Th1 differentiation; the resulting IFN-gamma activates macrophages to kill the intracellular mycobacteria, the core of cell-mediated defense.
explanation.whyCorrect: IL-12 from infected macrophages activates NK cells and drives Th1 differentiation; the resulting IFN-gamma activates macrophages to kill the intracellular mycobacteria, the core of cell-mediated defense.

questions.stem: This overview schematic contrasts primary and secondary tuberculosis outcomes. In an immunocompetent person, what is the most common outcome after primary infection?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Containment within a latent granuloma with no symptoms

explanation.summary: In roughly 90% of immunocompetent patients, primary infection is contained within a latent granuloma, and the patient remains asymptomatic for years.
explanation.whyCorrect: In roughly 90% of immunocompetent patients, primary infection is contained within a latent granuloma, and the patient remains asymptomatic for years.

questions.stem: This three-panel schematic illustrates the reaction underlying a positive PPD test over 24-72 hours. Which cells are the principal mediators of the visible lesion that develops?

explanation.summary: The delayed-type (type IV) reaction is driven by sensitized T cells that recognize injected antigen and recruit macrophages, producing the palpable induration.
explanation.whyCorrect: The delayed-type (type IV) reaction is driven by sensitized T cells that recognize injected antigen and recruit macrophages, producing the palpable induration.
